author: @Jannie303
text: Excellent idea! I'm working on a design for wireless, solar security cameras and this will come in very handy. Are you sure all the LED's have the same current though? I'm a complete amateur at circuits but I remember Kirchoffs law about current into a junction==current out. I get the current going through the first LED to be the sum of the current through all the other LED's plus the current going through the first resistor. Please let me know what you think, I'm still learning circuits so would greatly appreciate the feedback.

author: @stevetobias4890
text: I am making a circuit currently that is a low voltage alert for my solar batteries so I know when to turn off my inverter that runs half the house. When the inverter is switched off I have an automatic switch that turns it over to mains. The circuit is running with an op amp running as a comparitor and a trimpot to set the desired circuit. When reached it sounds a 12v piezo buzzer and I turn off the inverter.

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text: Nice circuit. Current for individual leds will vary with the voltage when lit. That's not a big problem as long as you keep current within led specification. Different colors have different forward voltages so led color will be a factor in voltage threshold indicated. Even for a single type of led typically there's big variation in forward voltage between production batches ( let's say one V max) so it's hard to rely on that. I guess this is a good indicator for a lead acid battery as I expect remaining life is a function of voltage. This is very different for lithium batteries. Special fuel gauge IC's are used for those. These keep statistics on battery performance and measure temperature to indicate what percentage is left. That's what's in your phone for example.

author: @DiyEcoProjects
text: Thats fun... Hi there Luke. how would you go about setting it for a range between 11-13vols say? as we know any car battery below 11 would get knacked. If its set to 11-13 Volts, you can also use it on solar panels coming in.

replies.author: @Phantom-mk4kp
replies.text: Cant be done with this circuit, every time a LED goes out the V has dropped another 2V approx. would need a more complex circuit, a transistor amplifier at least

replies.author: @Earzone63
replies.text: @Phantom-mk4kp can't you just extend this same circuit i.e. currently (no pun) the voltage divide is 12/(1 to 5), with ~3.6V steps, but with a (1 to x) range you'll get smaller steps, even if the number of lit LEDs e.g. 9 out 10 doesn't correspond to 90%, but that's where colour choice can come in (because colour is also a source of information ;)

replies.author: @DiyEcoProjects
replies.text: oh ok, thank you both for your replies :)

author: @reaneccles754
text: Thank you Luke, I follow you as I do Robert, you are doing fab!

author: @AndyFletcherX31
text: The steps are a bit coarse for a lead acid battery as you need to go between 11.1V (1.85V/cell) to 13.8V (2.30V/cell).  A quad comparator circuit like the LM339 hanging off a  resistor string driving the LEDs is probably a better solution.

replies.author: @Teknopottu
replies.text: True. And 12V is pretty low on lead acid battery, would not go lower than that.
